evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go defines action identity, ownership, and upload payload constraints in code-level constants and constructors.
goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go separately states the same upload payload governance policy as prose documentation.

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go and goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go both enforce overlapping registry/payload invariants from different package perspectives, indicating policy enforcement is distributed rather than centralized.

Dependency handoffs are dominated by large callback bags instead of focused collaborators (e.g., `importerRuntime` embeds storage/upload/provision callback groups in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, and `Runtime*Ops` families in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`).

Startup orchestration composes many grouped callback structs with runtime-tag validation and optional/required policy branches, making integration seams wide and hard to reason about (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`).

Cross-module integration still depends on process-global defaults (`DefaultEventRuntime`, `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime`) rather than explicit per-composition ownership (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`, `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`).

Core control paths are obscured by repetitive nil-guard/validation plumbing around function fields instead of direct domain logic (`goseg/main.go`, `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`).

Mutable package globals hold runtime behavior and state (`uploadSessions`, `uploadDir`, `tempDir`, `broadcastInterval`, singleton controllers/runtimes), requiring external reset choreography and reducing local reasoning (`goseg/importer/importer.go`, `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`, `goseg/leak/broadcast.go`, `goseg/leak/broadcast_test.go`).

State and lifecycle concerns are split across multiple package globals and locks, which increases incidental complexity in otherwise simple update/broadcast flows (`goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `goseg/leak/broadcast.go`).
No deprecated markers, legacy TODOs, or old/new API dual-path comments appear in the five scoped files.
Only one file (goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go) references governance location explicitly; other package docs present strong behavioral guarantees without migration/status breadcrumbs.
The scoped files are mostly package-level docs, so migration completion is asserted indirectly rather than through executable-path evidence.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: Migration state is not explicitly documented across related package docs, so future legacy cleanup boundaries are hard to audit from these entry-point files alone.

evidence: goseg/routines/version.go assumes every reflected field in version structs (except groundseg) is structs.VersionDetails and performs unchecked assertions at lines 420-421, so control flow can panic instead of returning an error when schema changes.

The version subsystem is wired as a startup background service in goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go (e.g., StartVersionFn mapping), so this panic path sits on a continuously running core path.
goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go exposes UpdateUrbitSectionFn as func(any) error, which erases section-specific type guarantees at runtime boundary.

goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go persists section updates through persistShipUrbitSection(..., mutateFn func(any) error), propagating the untyped seam across workflow code and relying on runtime adaptation instead of compile-time checking.
goseg/broadcast/state.go GetStateJson encodes auth_level from transition.BroadcastAuthLevel values like authorized/unauthorized.
goseg/leak/broadcast.go repurposes AuthLevel for scoped unauthorized payloads by setting AuthLevel to the ship identifier (~patp), changing field meaning from authorization class to identity token.
Tests in goseg/broadcast/state_test.go assert enum-style auth_level values, while goseg/leak/broadcast_test.go asserts patp in the same field, confirming competing contracts.
goseg/importer/importer.go validateUploadRequest authenticates session+token but takes patp as an external parameter and sets validated.Patp from that parameter without binding it to session state.
The session model in goseg/importer/importer.go (uploadSession) stores token/drive flags but no authorized patp, so identity and resource target are separated.
goseg/importer/importer.go toUploadImportCommand forwards validated.Patp into ship import orchestration, making target ship selection dependent on unbound request input after token validation.

findings.summary: Action-contract governance is split across catalog code, service docs, and duplicated cross-package tests, so ownership is non-local and changes require ripple edits.
evidence: Upload payload rules are encoded in UploadActionSpecs() (RequiredPayloads/ForbiddenPayloads) in the protocol catalog.
The same behavioral policy is repeated textually in uploadsvc/doc.go instead of being generated from the canonical spec.
Both conformance and uploadsvc tests independently assert contract alignment and payload-rule behavior, creating parallel enforcement paths tied to the same policy.
findings.suggestion: Promote a single canonical manifest API for action contracts (identity, ownership, payload rules) and generate dependent artifacts from it (service-facing docs, fixture expectations, and shared test helpers) so policy changes are authored once and consumed everywhere.

findings.summary: Integration seams are over-expanded into callback mega-structs, creating high indirection cost and brittle cross-module handoffs.
evidence: `importerRuntime` composes many unrelated function seams (storage/upload/provision/publish) and then requires stage-specific validation functions, which spreads dependency contract logic across many helpers instead of one boundary.

`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`, etc. in `runtime_ops.go` expose broad operation surfaces that couple consumers to a large shared bag even when only a small subset is needed.

`StartupRuntime` and nested startup runtime structs use many callback fields plus grouped validation and optional-policy execution, which makes orchestration read as callback wiring more than composition of cohesive services.
findings.suggestion: Replace function-field bags with smaller role-specific interfaces or concrete collaborators per workflow stage (e.g., ImportStorageService, ImportProvisionService, StartupBackgroundServiceRegistry), and inject only what each stage needs. Keep one validation boundary per composed service graph instead of per group.

findings.summary: Cross-module communication relies on mutable process-wide singletons, hiding ownership and coupling unrelated flows through shared defaults.
evidence: `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime` / `SetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` keep broadcast state as a process-global mutable singleton.
`DefaultEventRuntime` / `SetDefaultEventRuntime` maintain global transition buses used implicitly by callers.
`StartDockerSubsystem` and health loops default to `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime`, and importer default wiring publishes through `events.DefaultEventRuntime()`.
findings.suggestion: Move singleton defaults to bootstrap-only compatibility adapters and pass explicit runtime instances (event broker, broadcast state runtime, docker subsystem runtime) through constructors/entrypoints. Enforce ownership in startup composition so module seams are explicit.

findings.summary: Package-level mutable variables hold live workflow state, forcing non-local reasoning and fragile test/runtime behavior.
evidence: `importer.go` stores active upload sessions and directories in package globals (`uploadSessions`, `uploadDir`, `tempDir`, `uploadTTL`) shared across operations.
`leak/broadcast.go` uses shared package maps/channels for connection state, while tests must manually reset package globals (`resetLeakStateForTest`) to recover isolation.
`broadcast/loop.go` uses global mutable loop controls (`broadcastInterval`, `defaultBroadcastLoopController`) rather than instance-local scheduler state.
findings.suggestion: Encapsulate mutable workflow state in explicit runtime instances passed through APIs; keep package globals immutable (constants or constructor-initialized defaults only). Replace test reset helpers with per-test runtime construction.

findings.summary: Function internals are cluttered by repeated nil-guard and fallback scaffolding, which dilutes intent and expands branch surface.
evidence: `main.go` repeats callback nil checks across `validate` and each runtime method (`bootstrap`, `startServer`, `runC2CCheck`) and additional nested runtime-context fallbacks.
`startup_orchestrator.go` repeatedly checks callback presence in step execution and runtime-start task loops, duplicating policy/error shaping in multiple places.
`importer.go` splits runtime requirement checks into several near-identical validators and requirement selectors, adding ceremony before core workflow logic.
findings.suggestion: Normalize runtimes once at construction (fill defaults + enforce required contracts) and keep execution paths branch-light. Consolidate repeated nil-check blocks into shared constructor/validator utilities so operational methods focus on domain behavior.

findings.summary: Package docs embed enforceable runtime guarantees without local enforcement anchors
evidence: Comments state strict behavior guarantees (e.g., phase ordering, error normalization, privileged side-effect sequencing), but these files expose no nearby symbol references that maintainers can follow to verify enforcement.
Policy-heavy phrasing is repeated across modules with different style/detail levels, increasing drift risk when implementation changes.
Only uploadsvc doc points to governance documentation; sibling docs with equally strict claims do not provide matching traceability.
findings.suggestion: Standardize package-doc structure across these modules: keep short scope statement in doc.go, move enforceable behavioral guarantees to symbol-level docs/tests, and add explicit references (function/type names or contract constant locations) so each guarantee is traceable and updateable.

findings.summary: Ship workflow package boundary is blurred by mixing transport payload handling and domain provisioning orchestration.
evidence: HandleNewShip and HandleNewShipBoot process websocket-specific payload/action strings while also driving lifecycle orchestration and transition-event side effects.

newShipProvisioningRuntime wires infra collaborators directly from multiple packages into workflow runtime assembly, so transport-triggered entrypoints and provisioning composition share the same package surface.
findings.suggestion: Split shipworkflow into explicit boundary layers: move websocket payload/action parsing into a transport adapter package and keep shipworkflow focused on typed domain commands plus orchestration contracts.

findings.summary: Config package acts as a catch-all for runtime context control and persisted settings ownership, weakening directory boundary clarity.
evidence: config.go exposes context mutation APIs (SetBasePath/SetDebugMode/etc.) and context snapshots in the same package that defines multiple domain settings DTOs.

bootstrap.go performs disk/config bootstrap and global config state initialization while relying on the same package-level context APIs, combining process-context concerns with config persistence lifecycle.
findings.suggestion: Separate process runtime-context management from persisted system-config management into distinct packages (for example `config/runtimecontext` facade as sole owner of context mutators, `config/store` for disk-backed config lifecycle), then keep `config` as a narrow integration facade.

findings.summary: Top-level orchestration package is overloaded with container adapter wiring details, creating unstable ownership boundaries.
evidence: container_bridge.go defines a large callback bag and mapping logic for multiple container runtimes (llama, netdata, minio), indicating detailed adapter composition lives in the root orchestration layer.

container_config.go keeps builder registry and container-type dispatch in the same package, so adding/changing container wiring requires edits in central orchestration files rather than container-local modules.
findings.suggestion: Move runtime-bridge and per-container config builders into container-scoped subpackages (or an internal adapters layer) and keep root orchestration focused on high-level lifecycle operations plus minimal dispatch interfaces.

findings.summary: Contract catalogs use inconsistent ownership modeling, weakening a single architecture contract vocabulary.
evidence: `action/specs.go` defines `type OwnerModule string` and converts it to `Owner` fields, but `startram/specs.go` bypasses that pattern with raw `OwnerStartram` string.
README positions contract governance as a canonical architecture concern, but owner semantics are not represented through one shared contract metadata abstraction in the reviewed files.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one shared ownership contract type/registry in protocol contracts (used by both action and startram catalogs), and enforce its usage through conformance tests so all families share the same ownership schema.

findings.summary: Documented governance scope is broader than what the reviewed contract-conformance tests actually enforce.
evidence: README declares backend contract gate coverage that includes family governance and policy-level conformance.

In `fixtures_test.go`, tests cover fixture-to-binding parity and upload payload-rule validity, but do not assert family-level ownership/compatibility invariants described as governance concerns in docs.
`uploadsvc/doc.go` points to central governance policy, but corresponding explicit policy assertions are not visible in the reviewed conformance suite.
findings.suggestion: Translate governance-policy claims into explicit conformance tests (for example owner-domain validity and family policy invariants) or narrow README wording to exactly match executable checks.

findings.summary: Contract-governance verification is split between conformance and service tests, creating unclear ownership of the quality gate.
evidence: `fixtures_test.go` validates upload binding specs and fixture mapping at protocol-contract level.
`actions_test.go` also performs governance-style parity checks against `contracts.UploadActionBindingSpecs()` and repeats payload-rule expectation logic in uploadsvc tests.
README frames governance as a shared quality gate, but there is no single obvious canonical test boundary in these files.
findings.suggestion: Define a single canonical governance test boundary (protocol/contracts conformance suite) and keep uploadsvc tests focused on runtime behavior; move shared policy assertions into reusable conformance helpers consumed consistently by both layers.

findings.summary: Version comparison logic relies on unchecked reflection assertions that can panic on schema drift.
evidence: goseg/routines/version.go lines 420-421 cast reflected fields directly to structs.VersionDetails with .(structs.VersionDetails) and no ok-check/error branch.
The startup orchestrator registers the version subsystem as a long-lived background service, so a panic here affects operational stability beyond a local helper.
findings.suggestion: Replace reflection assertions with explicit typed iteration (or checked type assertions returning structured errors), and fail closed with a recoverable error path when version schema mismatches are detected.

findings.summary: Urbit section mutation contracts are stringly/untyped at module boundaries, allowing runtime type mismatches.
evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go defines UpdateUrbitSectionFn with mutateFn func(any) error.
goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go persistShipUrbitSection accepts func(any) error and passes it through runtime boundary, so section/mutator compatibility is not compile-time enforced.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed section mutation interfaces (per UrbitConfigSection) and replace func(any) error with section-specific generic or interface contracts so invalid mutators fail at compile time.

findings.summary: The broadcast auth_level field has conflicting semantics (authorization class vs ship identity) across producers.
evidence: goseg/broadcast/state.go sets auth_level from BroadcastAuthLevel enum values.
goseg/leak/broadcast.go sets AuthLevel to patp for unauthorized scoped payloads.
Associated tests assert both meanings in different codepaths, confirming the contract split.
findings.suggestion: Split identity and authorization into separate fields (for example auth_level + scope_subject), migrate both producers/consumers to the new envelope, and keep backward compatibility via an explicit versioned contract shim.

findings.summary: Upload token/session authorization is validated independently from target ship identity, enabling inconsistent resource authorization.
evidence: goseg/importer/importer.go uploadSession has no patp field, and validateUploadRequest sets validated.Patp from handler parameter rather than session-bound identity.
goseg/importer/importer.go toUploadImportCommand forwards validated.Patp to workflow execution, so final target selection depends on unbound request input after auth validation.
findings.suggestion: Bind patp (or allowed patp set) into uploadSession during endpoint open, verify request patp matches bound identity in validateUploadRequest, and reject mismatch before any workflow dispatch.
